antiPatterns.statement: Do not dump policy, methodology, persona, routing rules, and workflow logic into a single giant copilot-instructions.md. This creates massive token drag and makes every prompt pay for content only some tasks need.
Do not stuff prompts or agents with full procedural playbooks when a skill should carry the method. Prompts should be thin entrypoints; methodology belongs in skills.
Do not reward wording that sounds authoritative but does not specify inputs, boundaries, and verification expectations. Confident-sounding vague instructions lead to unpredictable agent behavior.
Do not over-privilege agents by giving them all available tools. An agent with unrestricted tool access can make destructive changes. Always apply least-privilege tool selection.

Do not publish workspace-specific context, private paths, or repository descriptions into a public cache. Even when a conclusion looks generalizable, strip all identifying local details before sharing.
Do not use the deprecated 'mode' field in .prompt.md frontmatter. Use 'agent' instead. The 'mode' field silently prevents the slash command from registering.
Do not use settings-based instructions (settings.json github.copilot.chat.codeGeneration.instructions). This was deprecated in VS Code 1.102 in favor of file-based .instructions.md.
Do not hardcode model names in .agent.md or .prompt.md unless the workflow truly requires a specific model. Model availability changes; hard-coded model assumptions age quickly and break silently.
Do not create agents without stopping rules. An agent without explicit exit conditions may loop indefinitely, consuming premium requests. Always define when the agent should stop working and yield.
Do not let agents edit hook scripts without manual approval. If the agent can modify its own hooks, it can circumvent safety policies. Use chat.tools.edits.autoApprove to protect hook files.

Do not create a skill for a one-time operation. Skills have overhead (folder structure, SKILL.md, discovery/routing). Use inline instructions or a simple prompt file for operations that won't be repeated.

Do not duplicate the same guidance across multiple .instructions.md files. If multiple files need the same rule, put it in copilot-instructions.md (always-on) or create a shared instruction file with a broad applyTo glob.

Do not write instructions that restate language fundamentals ('use meaningful variable names', 'handle errors'). The model already knows these. Focus on project-specific conventions the model cannot infer.
Do not mix tool name formats between Claude Code and VS Code in hook scripts. Claude uses snake_case (file_path) while VS Code uses camelCase (filePath). VS Code ignores Claude matchers entirely.

Do not write agents that attempt to do everything. Split complex workflows into multiple agents with handoffs. A planning agent should hand off to an implementation agent, not try to plan and implement simultaneously.
Do not hardcode secrets in hook scripts. Use environment variables or secure credential storage. Hook scripts run with the same permissions as VS Code — a leaked secret has full filesystem access.
Do not use Stop hooks without checking stop_hook_active. A Stop hook that always blocks creates an infinite loop where the agent never terminates, consuming unlimited premium requests.
Do not assume one repository's Copilot setup is the platform standard. Extract patterns that recur across multiple repos and docs. A single repo's approach may be project-specific or even outdated.

Do not fill domain-specific instruction files with rules the toolchain already enforces. Formatting rules, unused import detection, and basic type safety checks are linter, formatter, or compiler territory. Repeating them in instruction files wastes context tokens, signals low-effort content, and dilutes the rules that actually require judgment.

Do not prescribe a design pattern without explaining its tradeoffs and counterindications. Prescribing patterns without context causes over-application of abstractions in situations they were not designed for. Instruction files that include pattern guidance must also specify the signals that indicate the pattern is NOT the right choice.

Do not conflate architecture documentation with instruction files. A system architecture document that describes what the codebase IS belongs in docs. An instruction file must describe what the AI should DO differently in this codebase. Document-style writing in instruction files produces passive, descriptive content the model reads as background context, not as active guidance.

Do not treat instruction file content as a one-time setup artifact. AI-generated code reflects the instruction file content over time. If the codebase has evolved beyond what the instruction files describe, the AI generates code that fits the old state. Instruction file content has a maintenance lifecycle just like documentation — review it when significant architectural or domain knowledge changes occur.
